title: Cascaded Four-Wave Mixing on Quantum Paraelectrics for On-chip Cryogenic Microcombs
authors: Harikrishnan Sundaresan, Prasad Muragesh, Madhu Thalakulam
abstract: Here, we demonstrate an on-chip cryogenic microwave frequency-comb on a planar superconducting resonator fabricated on Strontium titanate(STO), a quantum paraelectric material. The material's Kerr-type nonlinearity arising from the quantum paraelectric phase, an underexplored state, enables pronounced resonance shifts, Duffing-like bifurcations and comb generation via cascaded foure-wave mixing. Our results establish STO resonators as a low power, cryogenically compatible platforms for nonlinear microwave photonics, with applications in scalable quantum control and on-chip frequency synthesis.
summary: This paper demonstrates a new method for generating broadband, phase-coherent microwave frequency combs using the natural nonlinearity of Strontium Titanate substrates. By using two microwave pump tones, the researchers created a comb with over 1000 lines that is highly tunable and stable. This technology could significantly reduce the complexity of control electronics for large-scale superconducting quantum computers.
main_problem: The need for scalable, on-chip, and tunable cryogenic microwave frequency combs to replace bulky electronics for tasks like multiplexed control and readout in large-scale quantum processors.
main_result: Demonstrated the generation of a phase-coherent, broadband microwave frequency comb (>1000 lines) using the intrinsic Kerr nonlinearity of a Strontium Titanate (STO) substrate via cascaded four-wave mixing.
method: The researchers used dual-tone microwave pumping on a superconducting aluminum coplanar waveguide resonator at millikelvin temperatures and analyzed the nonlinear response via microwave reflection (S11) and time-domain pulse analysis.
model_or_system: A superconducting aluminum coplanar waveguide (CPW) resonator fabricated on a Strontium Titanate (STO) quantum paraelectric substrate, modeled as a nonlinear oscillator with a Kerr-type Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Resonance frequency shifts, self-Kerr coefficient, frequency comb bandwidth, Free Spectral Range (FSR), spectral flatness, and long-term phase stability.
important_parameters: Operating temperature ~600 mK, Kerr coefficient of -10.20e-11 Hz per photon, comb bandwidth >150 MHz, and tunable FSR from 10 kHz to 1 MHz.
main_assumptions: The resonator is assumed to be in an over-coupled regime, and the nonlinearity is primarily attributed to the third-order Kerr effect from soft transverse phonon modes in the STO.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic, the dual-tone pumping scheme, and the power-dependent resonance evolution; Figure 2 displays the broadband comb spectrum and its high spectral flatness; Figure 4 demonstrates the long-term phase stability of the comb lines.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for on-chip combs, describes the STO-based experimental platform, quantifies the intrinsic Kerr nonlinearity, demonstrates the generation of the broadband frequency comb, and concludes with stability and tunability analysis.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum control as it presents a new material platform for generating large-scale, phase-locked microwave photon states, potentially solving the 'cabling bottleneck' in quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
